Che Guevera

# Che Guevera

### Hero or villian?

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Feb 16, 2021

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

I would not call [Che Guevera](https://www.quora.com/Should-Che-Guevara-be-regarded-as-a-hero-or-a-villain/answer/Ratnakar-Sadasyula-%E0%B0%B0%E0%B0%A4%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%A8%E0%B0%BE%E0%B0%95%E0%B0%B0%E0%B1%8D-%E0%B0%B8%E0%B0%A6%E0%B0%B8%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%AF%E0%B1%81%E0%B0%B2) a hero or a villain, to me he was more of a flawed hero, idealistic, intelligent, great strategist, but at the same time, dictatorial, not pragmatic enough, impatient. Every human being has flaws you might say, but fact is Che's negative points were as overwhelming as his positive ones.

Che was intelligent and knowledgeable, he had an encyclopedic knowledge on philosophy, history, sciences, psychology. A true intellectual, who could hold forth on any topic be it dream theory or philosophy or economics. It was this intelligence that enabled him to be Fidel Castro's brain during the Cuban revolution. He not just used the revolutionaries as cannon fodder, he set up makeshift schools, educated the campesinos, set up health camps. He was the one who masterminded the guerilla attacks against Batista's troops, using strategy to fight against a much larger military force. If Cuba today boasts of one of the world's highest literacy rates, credit goes to Che, who created the literacy brigades, that spread out into the countryside.

But for all his intelligence and strategic genius though, Che's negative side was equally overwhelming. Intransigent and dictatorial in his approach, he executed countless people on suspicion of them being counter revolutionaries or deserters. He could not get along with people, whom he deemed to be lesser intelligent than him. For all his sympathies with the masses, Che essentially was an elitist, very much unlike Castro, who was comfortable with them. Castro was very much the man of the masses, the one who could sit down with them, have a cup of tea, Che on the other hand was seen as too aloof, too elitist in nature. His economic theories were hopelessly naive, no way one could ever even implement them. Che's ideas were too radical even for Castro and the Soviets, and it was not a surprise that he fell out with both of them later.

Also while Che's military strategies worked well in Latin America, he was an utter failure in Africa too. He could never really get along with what he called "lazy, undisciplined troops" in Africa.

As an individualistic, Che was one of the most erudite, knowledgeable, intellectual personalities. He had a genuine love and affection for the poor. However his intransigent, dictatorial nature would be his undoing. His mass executions of counter revolutionaries, deserters( people who were generally poor and oppressed), his purges of suspected betrayers, all had a heavy Stalin hangover. His major failing though, would be his intellectual arrogance, that alienated many from him.
